ground_truth.title: Guide To Hotel Management Companies for 2025
ground_truth.author: Josiah Mackenzie
ground_truth.main_content: This comprehensive guide explains what hotel management companies are and how to choose one for 2025. Hotel management companies manage the operations of hotels on behalf of owners, including hiring, training, marketing, finances, and ensuring quality and profitability. The guide includes interviews with experts including David Chu (SVP at GAM Hospitality), Mark Keiser (Chief Development Officer at EOS Hospitality), Scott Curran (COO at Reneson Hotels), Susan Barry (Principal at HIVE), and Brian Proctor (Principal at Leeds Hospitality Group). Experts explain the hotel ecosystem where brands license names, ownership groups invest in real estate, and hotel management companies handle day-to-day operations. Management companies typically charge 3-5% of revenue or gross operating income. Contracts usually run 3-5 years. The guide lists the 10 largest US hotel management companies by property count: Aimbridge Hospitality (1,500+ hotels), Hotel Equities (250+ hotels), HHM (185+ hotels), Pyramid Global Hospitality (220 hotels), Highgate, Concord Hospitality Enterprises, GF Hotels and Resorts, TPG Hotels, Davidson Hospitality Group, and Crestline Hotels & Resorts (123 hotels). Tips for choosing a management company include checking experience with your brand type, reviewing track record, understanding fee structures, verifying reputation, and meeting the team to ensure alignment on goals.
with: Hotel management companies manage the operations of a hotel or group of hotels on behalf of the hotel owners.
Management companies typically make money by charging a percentage of the hotel's revenue as a management fee, with 3% of topline revenue being a good rule of thumb.
Aimbridge is the biggest hotel management company by property count, with more than 1,500 hotels in over 20 countries.

When choosing a hotel management company, consider experience with the hotel type, brand, and location, track record of performance, range of services offered, fees and contract structure, and reputation and references.
Third-party hotel management companies are there to protect the owner's asset value while maintaining brand standards.
